section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: Cross-lingual information retrieval (CLIR) consists in finding relevant documents in a language that differs from the language of the queries. This paper presents CLIRudit, a new dataset created to evaluate cross-lingual academic search, focusing on English queries and French documents. The dataset is built using bilingual article metadata from Érudit, a Canadian publishing platform, and is designed to represent scenarios in which researchers search for scholarly content in languages other than English. We perform a comprehensive benchmarking of different zero-shot first-stage retrieval methods on the dataset, including dense and sparse retrievers, query and document machine translation, and state-of-the-art multilingual retrievers. Our results show that large dense retrievers, not necessarily trained for the cross-lingual retrieval task, can achieve zero-shot performance comparable to using ground truth human translations, without the need for machine translation. Sparse retrievers, such as BM25 or SPLADE, combined with document translation, show competitive results, providing an efficient alternative to large dense models. This research advances the understanding of cross-lingual academic information retrieval and provides a framework that others can use to build comparable datasets across different languages and disciplines. By making the dataset and code publicly available, we aim to facilitate further research that will help make scientific knowledge more accessible across language barriers.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: • Information systems $$ Multilingual and cross-lingual retrieval; Retrieval models and ranking.
cross-lingual information retrieval, academic search, multilingual embeddings, machine translation, evaluation resources
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: Cross-lingual information retrieval (CLIR) consists in finding relevant documents in a language that differs from the language of the queries. By removing the need for users to be proficient in multiple languages to find information, it makes valuable content accessible regardless of the language in which it was originally created, reducing the risk of missing relevant content due to language limitations.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: Cross-lingual capability is particularly critical in academic search, where important research contributions may be published in languages other than English. While English is the dominant language of international scientific communication, valuable academic content is often only available in other languages, especially in certain disciplines and historical contexts [5, 21, 42]. Researchers may miss important work if they cannot effectively search across language boundaries, particularly when dealing with specialized terminology. While researchers may be familiar with technical terms in
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: English, they may struggle to formulate effective queries using the appropriate specialized vocabulary in other languages. The use of English as the default language may also be driven by the expectation of finding more results, perpetuating a bias against the discoverability of potentially relevant documents in other languages. This pattern is reflected in the default CLIR task setting, where queries are expressed in English, and the documents are in another language [24, 25].
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: Modern information retrieval (IR) approaches rely on dense embeddings as representations of text [12], typically within a biencoder architecture [20, 59]. Cross-lingual embeddings [1, 2, 10] have shown promise for general domain CLIR tasks [3, 62], and machine translation (MT) approaches that convert queries and/or documents into a common language have also been explored [30]. While previous work has shown the utility of translation in general domain CLIR [3, 30], its effectiveness for academic content remains underexplored.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: Our study compares different first-stage retrieval approaches to investigate their effectiveness in the domain of scholarly communication. We explore several key questions: Can dense embeddings alone support effective CLIR in this context? Which types of embeddings perform best? Does incorporating MT improve retrieval, and if so, is it preferable to translate queries or documents?
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: We address these questions and make the following contributions:

• We provide a generalizable methodology for constructing academic CLIR datasets by leveraging the multilingual metadata (titles, abstracts, keywords) available in some scientific publications. This methodology can be applied to other academic databases and language pairs, facilitating research in cross-lingual scientific information access.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: We build and publicly release CLIRudit1, a novel dataset for academic CLIR, consisting of English queries and French documents, based on Érudit, a non-profit publishing platform based in Quebec, Canada.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: • We present a comprehensive empirical comparison of first-stage retrieval methods for academic CLIR in our dataset. Our evaluation includes query translation, document translation, and a wide range of state-of-the-art retrieval methods, providing evidence-based insights into the effectiveness of each approach in the academic search setting.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: Our findings show that state-of-the-art dense embeddings without translation achieve the best overall performance in a zero-shot setting. However, using sparse retrievers, which allows for efficient search with inverted indexes, proves to be competitive when combined with document translation, and even outperforms many dense multilingual embeddings (see Figure 1).

These results provide a valuable benchmark for future research in CLIR and have significant implications for improving the discoverability of research published in languages other than English in scientific information retrieval systems. In particular, this work has direct implications for platforms such as Érudit that focus on scientific literature in languages other than English, as it suggests ways to improve the discoverability of their documents.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: In this section, we present an overview of the relevant research for academic CLIR, reviewing first-stage retrieval approaches, datasets, and academic bilingual corpora.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: Lin et al. [30] provided a conceptual framework for organizing different CLIR approaches, identifying three main strategies for first-stage retrieval, that is, the initial retrieval of $k \cdot$ documents from a collection given a query:
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text:
document translation: translating all documents in the collection to the query language before retrieval.   
• query translation: translating the query to the language of the documents prior to retrieval.   
• using language-independent representations to encode queries and documents in a shared embedding space, which allows direct retrieval without translation.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: They also highlighted the potential of using cross-lingual rerankers built on existing multilingual models to improve the ranking of first-stage retrieval candidates, as well as the benefits of results fusion of different retrieval methods. However, in this work, we focus exclusively on first-stage retrieval.

Even if the effectiveness of these approaches varies across different domains and language pairs, translation-based methods have been shown to be overall effective. Lin et al. [30] found that query or document translation followed by monolingual retrieval with sparse learned representations such as SPLADE [14] have promising results in the general-domain NEUCLIR 2022 dataset [24], while Asai et al. [3] found that translation of queries to English followed by English monolingual retrieval generally outperform multilingual approaches in Wikipedia data across seven languages. Similarly, Huang et al. [16] conducted extensive experiments on seven language pairs in a news dataset, including both low-resource and high-resource languages, confirming the effectiveness of query translation for high-resource languages like French. In the medical domain, Saleh and Pecina [47] found that query translation generally outperforms document translation for CLIR in patient-centered tasks.

The relationship between translation quality and retrieval effectiveness has also been a subject of research. In the pre -neural era, Zhu and Wang [65] found a high correlation between translation quality and retrieval effectiveness in translation-based CLIR. However, this correlation may not hold for all domains and scenarios, as evidenced by the findings of Pecina et al. [41], which show that better translation does not necessarily lead to better retrieval performance in the medical domain.

More recent work has also explored the use of embeddings in CLIR. Jiang et al. [19] showed the effectiveness of multilingual BERT embeddings without relying on translation, while Rosa et al. [44] demonstrated that for monolingual retrieval, particularly in low-resource scenarios, translating examples to English and using models fine-tuned on English data can outperform models specifically fine-tuned on the target language.

Building diverse and well-documented datasets is crucial for advancing research in CLIR, as it enables training and benchmarking systems across different language pairs and domains. Several approaches have been proposed to automatically generate such datasets.

For example, Mayfield et al. [33] developed a method for generating synthetic training data by pairing similar documents from a collection in a given target language, and then using a generative large language model (LLM) to generate English queries that are relevant to one document but not the other.

Several works take advantage of Wikipedia to create CLIR datasets because it contains articles in multiple languages in a structured format. Sasaki et al. [48] created a CLIR dataset by using the first sentence of English Wikipedia articles as queries, and retrieving corresponding foreign language documents using inter-language links. Similarly, Li et al. [28] used English sentences as queries and foreign language summaries as documents, focusing only on ambiguous terms to create a dataset that emphasizes disambiguation in retrieval tasks. By combining expert human translation and annotation with multilingual data from Wikidata, Longpre et al. [32] built a fully parallel evaluation set with the same examples in 26 languages. Finally, Sun and Duh [51] used Wikipedia titles as queries, BM25 as a method to find relevant documents, and Wikidata links between documents in different languages as bilingual relevance judgments. Ogundepo et al. [39] extended this approach to create a dataset with English queries and documents in 15 African languages.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: Although there are no CLIR datasets specifically focused on academic search at the time of writing, there are several parallel academic corpora comparable to the one we use.

SciPar [45] is a collection of bilingual titles and abstracts coming from theses doctoral dissertations mined from institutional repositories and national archives. Examples of academic parallel mentioned in Roussis et al. [45] include SciELO [37], with aligned sentences across English, Portuguese, and Spanish; ASPEC [35], with sentence pairs in English, Japanese, and Chinese; CAPES [50], based on Brazilian theses and dissertations; and EDP [36], a parallel corpus of biomedical articles in French and English. In the biomedical domain, MEDLINE [58] and BVS [49] provide aligned titles and abstracts in various languages, respectively. More recently, Niu and Jiang [38] presented a corpus containing translated academic abstracts from leading journals in translation studies.

As these corpora are primarily focused on machine translation in the domain of scholarly communication, they provide parallel abstracts and/or titles, often with sentence-level alignment. Here, we propose using keywords as queries as a novel approach to create a dataset for CLIR in academic search. Among these corpora, only CAPES and BVS include multilingual keywords as metadata, making them suitable for creating a dataset similar to ours.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: To evaluate methods for the academic CLIR task, we built CLIRudit using data from Érudit2, a Canadian platform based in Quebec dedicated to the dissemination of research in the arts, humanities, and social sciences. The journals hosted on Érudit are selected by a scientific committee and meet the criteria of national scientific agencies, which ensures the quality and relevance of the content.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: The CLIRudit dataset follows a TREC-style structure (TExt Retrieval Conference, 56), with three components: queries, a collection of documents or corpus, and relevance judgments (“qrels”).
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: We focused exclusively on research articles, discarding other document types such as book reviews or editorial notes. Additionally, we kept only articles that contained both abstracts and keywords in both French and English. These are available when the authors have provided translations from one language to the other. The metadata we considered for each article comprised titles, subtitles, abstracts, and keywords. The dataset was constructed as follows (see Figure 2 for an overview):
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: Queries. We used an approach that uses the English keywords associated with each article as queries. Specifically, for each article we generated all possible three-keyword combinations. For example, if an article had the keywords $\{ A , B , C , D \}$ , the queries generated would be: ${ } ^ { \mathfrak { s } } A , B , C ^ { \mathfrak { s } }$ , $^ { \ast } A , B , D ^ { \prime }$ ”, ${ } ^ { \ast } A , C , D ^ { \ast }$ , and $^ { \ast } B , C , D ^ { \ast }$ .

• Relevance judgments. A document was considered relevant to a query if its English keywords metadata contained all the keywords present in the query. This method is consistent with the assumption that authors want their articles to be discoverable by researchers searching for those specific keywords.
• Document collection. Each document in the corpus consists of the concatenated French title, subtitle, and abstract. This representation is treated as the retrieval unit.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: We chose a combination of three keywords for queries based on preliminary observations. Using fewer keywords resulted in overly broad queries with likely many false negatives. For example, using two keywords resulted in queries such as “family dynamics, gender identity” or “canada, québec” that are too general and could likely be relevant to several documents whose authors did not include these keywords. On the other hand, using more than three keywords resulted in overly specific queries that were very different from what a user would be expected to search for in a realistic environment.

The final dataset contains 357,710 queries derived from 41,594 distinct English keywords, with an average query length of 4.8 words $\mathrm { \langle S D = 1 . 7 \rangle }$ ; and 16,389 French documents from 124 journals of 25 disciplines, with an average document length of 176.7 words $\mathrm { \langle S D = } 8 2 . 4 \AA \AA \mathrm { ) }$ . Because of the way our dataset was built, all documents in the collection are relevant to at least one query. $9 9 . 3 \%$ of the queries have only one relevant document, meaning that a very small fraction of combinations of three keywords are shared by more than one document.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: $8 4 . 9 \%$ of the abstracts in the dataset come from articles whose primary language is French, $1 4 . 3 \%$ from English, and $0 . 9 \%$ from other languages. The most common disciplines from queries in the dataset are Humanities and Social Sciences, Water and Environment, Sociology, and Anthropology and Ethnology (see the full distribution in Figure 3).
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: CLIRudit aims to simulate the scenario where a user only knows the relevant terms in English, but relevant documents may only be available in French, without a direct translation of the document being available. The pipeline we used provides a reproducible method for constructing CLIR datasets for academic search that does not rely on complex heuristics, but on the structure of scientific publications themselves. While this work focuses on French-English retrieval, the methodology can be extended to other databases and language pairs, facilitating research in cross-lingual scientific information access.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: In this section, we describe the retrieval methods, machine translation systems, and evaluation metrics used to benchmark the performance of the different approaches in the dataset.

For performing first-stage retrieval, we considered diverse retrieval methods spanning lexical, sparse, and dense approaches [29]. Our selection criteria prioritized well-documented, open-source models that can be deployed at scale without costly proprietary APIs. Given the manageable size of our document collection, we performed exhaustive nearest-neighbor search in all cases. Details about the retrievers selected are provided in Table 1.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: Dense multilingual retrievers. Ideally, these models should support direct French-English cross-lingual retrieval without translation, as their pretraining and fine-tuning incorporate both French and English data. We considered three state-of-the-art bi-encoders that compute dense vector representations of queries and documents in a shared multilingual embedding space: $\mathbf { m } \mathbf { E } 5 ^ { 3 }$ [57], mGTE-dense4 [64], and BGE-M3-dense5 [7]. It’s important to note that while mGTE-dense and BGE-M3-dense include some cross-lingual tasks in their fine-tuning or pre-training data, where multiple languages appear within the same instance, mE5 is trained only on multilingual data, i.e. it is trained on multiple languages, but with samples that are monolingual. This may affect its performance in CLIR.

Dense English retrievers. Models designed primarily for English retrieval can be applied in two ways: (1) retrieving French documents after translating them to English, or (2) leveraging crosslingual transfer. Cross-lingual transfer occurs when models, finetuned mainly on one language (monolingual English retrieval in this case), demonstrate robust performance on other languages for the same task [2, 4, 62] (here, CLIR with English queries and French documents). We evaluated NV-Embed- $\mathbf { \cdot v 2 ^ { 6 } }$ [26], the top performer on the English MTEB retrieval benchmark [34] as of November 2024, and the second-best BGE-EN-ICL7 [27] in its zero-shot configuration i.e. without in-context examples appended to the queries. Both models require appending task-specific instructions before encoding the queries, which we do following the authors’ templates. While these models target English retrieval, they have actually been fine-tuned on some multilingual data, including French, which may improve cross-lingual generalization. Furthermore, the underlying language model, Mistral-7B-v0.1 [17], may have been exposed to multilingual pre-training data, but this information is not publicly available.

French-specialized dense retrievers. There is limited availability of dense retrievers specialized in languages other than English, and these tend to lack comprehensive documentation compared to their English counterparts, as they are mostly developed by open source communities. We considered three of these models that were high performing in the MTEB French benchmark [9] at the time of writing: Croissant8, based on the bilingual CroissantLLM [13], and $\mathbf { \delta s o l o n } ^ { 9 }$ and Lajavaness10, both initialized from multilingual encoders. Even if they are specialized in French, they are actually all bilingual at some degree as they include English data in pre-training or fine-tuning.

Sparse retrievers. Sparse models represent queries and documents using term-weighted vectors based on vocabulary tokens, enabling efficient retrieval through inverted indexing [14]. We used BM25 [43] with default parameters, a widely used exact-match method that is a strong baseline in IR [53]. In CLIR, BM25 is useful after translation of queries and documents into a common language, with an appropriate language-specific analyzer. More recently, learned sparse representations, where term weights are learned by training neural networks on supervised data, have proven to be more effective [30]. Among these is $\mathbf { s P L A D E + } + 1 1$ [23], which effectively performs implicit query and document expansion by assigning non-zero weights to relevant terms in the vocabulary that may not appear in the original query or document. However, $\mathrm { S P L A D E } + +$ is designed for monolingual English use and requires translation of documents to English. We also evaluated the sparse variants of the multilingual models mGTE and BGE-M3, namely mGTE-sparse [64] and BGE-M3-sparse [7]. These models enable direct cross-lingual retrieval but, unlike $\mathrm { S P L A D E } + +$ , do not perform term expansion. As a result, they only yield non-zero similarity scores when there are common tokens between the query and document, as in BM25, which may limit their effectiveness in CLIR.
We employed three machine translation models:

GPT-4o-mini12. Recent work has demonstrated the effectiveness of LLMs for document-level translation [22, 63]. We used a cost-efficient version of the proprietary OpenAI models, which have shown competitive performance on high-resource language pairs [15, 66].

• Llama-3.2. We used the 3.2B-parameter version as an opensource LLM alternative to GPT, with strong zero-shot capabilities in French to English translation [63]. Using open-source models can be advantageous for cost-efficiency and for the ability to fine-tune on domain-specific data.

• OpusMT, a lightweight French-English MarianMT encoder - decoder model [54, 55] of only 75M parameters trained on Opus parallel data13. While primarily designed for sentence-level translation, we applied it at the document level following recent work [11]. Moreover, it has a maximum input length of 512 tokens, significantly smaller than the $1 0 0 \mathbf { k } +$ token limits of GPT and Llama models.

For LLM-based translation, we used a zero-shot prompt inspired by established best practices for instruction-tuned LLMs . The complete prompt is provided in Table 2. We didn’t consider strong proprietary translators such as Google Translate or DeepL because they lack a cost-efficient API.

Finally, as an empirical upper bound, we used the actual English translations of the French titles, subtitles, and abstracts as documents. This upper bound represents the best possible performance that can be achieved by each retrieval method with perfect

You are a highly skilled translator from French to English.   
Your task is to accurately translate the French text I provide into English.   
You will be provided with a text, and you will output a JSON object containing the following information:   
{   
translation: string // the translated text   
$\}$   
Preserve the meaning, tone, and nuance of the original text.   
Please maintain proper grammar, spelling, and punctuation in the translated version.
Table 2: Prompt used for document translation with LLMs. We used a slight variation of this prompt for query translation.

translation. Alternatively, we could have used the actual French keywords as queries. However, there isn’t always a one-to-one mapping between the English and French keywords. This would mean that the set of queries would be different from the original English, which could introduce noise into the analysis.
We considered the following standard, complementary metrics to measure retrieval performance:

Recall ${ \mathcal { \mathbf { \mathbf { \rho } } } } ( { \mathcal { \mathbf { \mathbf { \mathit { a } } } } } ) \mathbf { k } \mathbf { : }$ the fraction of relevant documents within the top $k$ results, averaged over all queries. We used the capped version [53]. $\mathbf { n D C G } @ \mathbf { k } \colon$ : the normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain, measuring ranking quality by comparing the top $k$ results against an ideal ranking where relevant documents appear first. It is also averaged over all queries.

For ease of presentation in a table, we focus on Recall $@ 1 0 0$ and $\scriptstyle \mathrm { n D C G } @ 1 0$ as these cutoff points are widely adopted in the literature and are motivated by practical applications [53].

However, several other cut-off points may be relevant. In a pipeline without reranking, we may only be interested in the top 10 results; whereas in a system with reranking, we might be interested in the top 100 or 1000 results. Therefore, to provide a more comprehensive evaluation, we also present performance curves showing metrics at multiple cutoff points for specific analysis of interest (𝑘 ∈ {1, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 250, 500, 1000}). This allows us to verify if performance differences are robust across different operating points.

To evaluate translation quality at the document level, we employ three metrics that have been used in recent studies [52, 60, 67]: BLONDE [18], document-BLEU (d-BLEU, 31), and document-chrF (d-chrF, 67).

In this section, we analyze the performance of the different retrieval models and translation methods on CLIRudit. Table 3 shows the Recall@100 and nDCG@10 results for all models and methods. To account for differences in context length between models, we also evaluated using the same maximum sequence length of 512 tokens in the input of all methods. The results were nearly identical to those obtained with each model’s original maximum sequence length, with performance differences not exceeding 0.005.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: Below we provide observations and specific analyses based on the results.

1. Without translation, dense retrievers beat sparse retrievers, even without specific multilingual fine-tuning. NVEmbed-v2 is the best performing retriever, achieving the highest Recall $@ 1 0 0$ and $\mathrm { \ n D C G } @ 1 0$ , even if it is not reported to have multilingual capabilities (Figure 4). It should be noted, however, that while the NV-Embed-v2 retrieval fine-tuning data is exclusively in English, it does include French in cross-lingual sentence pairs from STS17 [6] and STS22 [8]. Solon, a dense retriever which does have French-specific fine-tuning, and BGE-EN-ICL, follow closely.

As expected, BM25, which employs the French analyzer, performs poorly without translation due to the language mismatch between queries and documents. However, the performance is not zero, showing there is some lexical overlap between the queries and the documents, particularly when queries include terms that are the same for both languages, such as proper nouns (e.g., some countries, or last names), Latin terms, and acronyms.

Compared to mGTE-sparse and BGE-M3-sparse, SPLADE $^ { + + }$ shows improved performance, which we hypothesize is due to its effective query expansion, which partially compensates for the language mismatch. Nevertheless, dense retrievers consistently outperform sparse ones without translation.

2. Translation has limited impact on dense retrievers. For dense retrievers, translation leads to performance improvements of up to $1 0 \%$ in Recall@k and nDCG@k when using GPT-4, the most effective translator overall (see Figure 5). However, the impact of translation on dense models is generally modest compared to sparse models, especially considering the top-performing NV-Embed-v2. In some cases, translation actually degrades the performance of dense retrievers, particularly when translating queries, as seen in models like mE5, BGE-EN-ICL, and Lajavaness.

A manual review of instances where query translation negatively impacts performance reveals that the translation model occasionally mistranslates certain proper nouns that are spelled identically in both languages, resulting in a loss of recall. For example, the keyword “Goose Bay” (a town in Canada) is translated as “Baie aux Oies”, when the correct translation should remain “Goose Bay”.

3. Document translation usually outperforms query translation for sparse retrievers. In sparse retrieval, translation always improves performance, with document translation to English consistently outperforming query translation to French (Figure 5).

This is particularly true for BM25 and $\mathrm { S P L A D E } + +$ , with $\mathrm { S P L A D E } + +$ approaching competitive performance against dense retrievers. While this is expected for SPLADE $^ { + + }$ since it is trained in English only, in the case of BM25, one hypothesis is that translating documents provides richer context for effective translation than translating queries, which are just a collection of keywords [30]. However, for mGTE-sparse and BGE-M3-sparse, the effect of translating queries and documents on retrieval is similar.

A manual inspection of cases where document translation outperforms query translation for BM25 reveals a French abstract containing the exact term “fair innings”, which remains correctly unchanged when the document is translated into English. However, translating the query to French results in the term “juste part” which is absent in the French document. Another example is the case of the keyword “beck” in the query “beck, cosmopolitanism, culture”. The French abstract provides enough content to understand that it refers to the surname of a social scientist, and therefore the document translation into English accurately identifies it as such. However, when the query is translated, the context is insufficient and the term $^ { \ast } b e c k ^ { \ast }$ is incorrectly translated as appel (French for “call”), rendering the relevant document irretrievable.

4. Document translation quality correlates with retrieval performance. The evaluation of the document translation quality reveals that GPT-4 achieves the highest scores across all metrics (BLEU $\stackrel { \cdot } { = } 3 4 . 4 1$ , BLONDE $\scriptstyle \_ { 1 } = 4 9 . 3 2$ , chrF $\scriptstyle \mathbf { \bar { \rho } } = 6 3 . 8 3$ ), followed closely by Llama (BLEU $\stackrel { \cdot } { = } 3 1 . 2 7$ , $\mathrm { B L O N D E } { = } 4 6 . 5 2$ , chrF $\scriptstyle \mathsf { \bar { \rho } } = 6 1 . 5 6$ ), while OpusMT lags significantly behind (BLEU: 10.77, BLONDE: 19.35, chrF: 36.15). This ranking aligns with the retrieval performance improvements we observed when using these translation systems, where GPT4 consistently outperformed Llama, which in turn outperformed OpusMT (see Table 3). While these results suggest a correlation between translation quality and retrieval performance, quantifying the precise impact of MT quality on CLIR performance would require additional experimentation beyond the scope of this paper.

5. Top methods approach their empirical monolingual upper bound. The highest-performing method, NV-Embed-v2, is very close to its empirical English upper bound that assumes perfect translation of documents, considering both recall and NDCG (Figure 6). For other models, there is a larger gap, suggesting that better translation could lead to improved retrieval performance. Interestingly, the Croissant bilingual retriever achieves better performance when queries are machine-translated to French than when using the ground truth English documents.

Notably, the sparse models BM25 and $\mathrm { S P L A D E } + +$ have a higher monolingual upper bound than NV-Embed-v2 for $\scriptstyle \mathrm { { n D C G } } \ @ 1 0$ (see Table 3), suggesting that improvements in translation quality could further close the gap with state-of-the-art dense retrieval models. However, given the nature of our dataset, where queries are keywords and documents are abstracts, it is not unexpected that BM25 and $\mathrm { S P L A D E } + +$ , which are based on lexical matching, perform well once the ground truth translations are available.

Our results highlight the strong performance of dense retrievers, such as NV-Embed-v2, which achieve effective cross-lingual retrieval without translation, performing almost as if they had direct access to the ground truth translated abstracts. Sparse retrievers, both traditional lexical and learned models such as $\mathrm { S P L A D E } + +$ , also show competitive results when combined with document translation. The advantage of this approach is that sparse retrieval can be efficiently implemented at scale with inverted indexes. Exploring the development of multilingual versions of learned sparse retrieval models such as SPLADE $^ { + + }$ may be a promising direction for future work.

Query translation generally yields lower performance than document translation, probably because queries lack the contextual information needed for accurate translation [30]. This also has practical limitations, as it does not allow control over user input at runtime, whereas document translation can be done offline, providing better control, which can be important when using proprietary, expensive models. However, query translation allows for faster experimentation, since new translation models can be tested without having to retranslate the entire corpus [30].

We found that the proprietary GPT4o-mini LLM outperformed the open-source Llama 3.2 and the smaller OpusMT encoder-decoder model in the zero-shot setting. However, further exploration with few-shot prompting or fine-tuning may improve the performance of the open-source models. In addition, OpusMT is not optimized for document-level translation, so using sentence-level translation may be more optimal. However, this approach requires a more complex pipeline with sentence splitting and risks losing contextual coherence across sentences.

Additionally, an important concern for fair evaluation of approaches is data contamination: the test data we use may be present in the training data of pre-trained models, especially LLMs used for translation and retrievers initialized from LLMs, such as NVEmbed-v2 and BGE-EN-ICL. This could lead to inflated results, but is difficult to verify due to the lack of information about the exact training data of these models [40, 46].

It is also important to note that the absolute retrieval performance values reported in this study may not be representative of real-world settings. The document collection in our dataset is limited to relevant documents, unlike in practical applications where relevant documents would coexist with a much larger collection. Therefore, the reported metrics should be used to compare methods rather than to provide absolute performance estimates, which is common in IR evaluations [53].

Our dataset may also suffer from false negatives, where relevant documents are not marked as such because some authors did not include some suitable keywords in the metadata, while others did. This is a known limitation in IR datasets, where relevance annotation is usually incomplete as it does not capture all relevant documents [61]. However, we believe that using three keywords per query mitigates this problem, as queries are less likely to be too broad, i.e. there is less likelihood that there is another document relevant to a query with three keywords besides the one in which the keywords are present.

A limitation of our study is that it only considers the metadata of the document, e.g. title, abstract. Future research could use techniques that deal with the full text to improve retrieval performance. In addition, the queries used in this study are keyword-based and may not represent natural language queries. Future work could explore the creation of more complex and natural queries, possibly with the help of annotators or LLMs. Our pipeline also uses a first-stage retrieval-only approach without re-ranking, fusion, or pseudo-relevance feedback [30]. Incorporating these methods could further improve performance and provide broader insights into the optimal design of CLIR architectures.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: Finally, we acknowledge that our study focuses on a high-resource language, French, and that results may vary in low-resource languages. In such contexts, translation quality and dense encoder performance may be lower due to limited training data. Future work should ideally evaluate retrieval performance in both lowresource and high-resource languages from different language families, allowing for a better understanding of the factors that influence performance in scholarly retrieval.

This work makes several contributions to cross-lingual academic search. First, we presented a methodology for constructing CLIR datasets that leverages the structure of scientific publications with bilingual metadata. This approach, which relies on keywords as queries and abstracts as documents, provides an automated and scalable way to create evaluation resources without the need for manual relevance annotation or complex heuristics. We demonstrated this methodology by creating CLIRudit, a French-English academic CLIR dataset based on content from Érudit, a Canadian academic publishing platform.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: Our comprehensive evaluation of retrieval methods on CLIRudit revealed several key findings: (1) state-of-the-art dense retrievers achieve strong cross-lingual performance without translation, approaching the performance of monolingual retrieval with ground truth translations; (2) sparse retrievers combined with document translation provide competitive performance while allowing efficient retrieval through inverted indexes; and (3) document translation generally outperforms query translation, especially for sparse retrievers, probably due to the richer context available for translation.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: These results have practical implications for academic search systems. While dense retrievers show superior performance, the strong results of sparse retrievers with document translation suggest a viable alternative that may be more practical to implement at scale. This is particularly relevant for platforms like Érudit that aim to make non-English academic content more discoverable to researchers.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: Beyond the specific French-English case studied here, our methodology can be extended to other academic databases and language pairs, providing a foundation for future research in cross-lingual scientific information access. This work ultimately contributes to the broader goal of improving the discoverability of valuable academic content across language boundaries.
section_title: CLIRudit: Cross-Lingual Information Retrieval of Scientific Documents

text: This project was funded by the Social Science and Humanities Research Council of Canada Pan-Canadian Knowledge Access Initiative Grant (Grant 1007-2023-0001), and the Fonds de recherche du Québec-Société et Culture through the Programme d’appui aux Chaires UNESCO (Grant 338828). We used computational resources from NodoIA San Francisco (Ministry of Science and Technology of the Province of Córdoba, Argentina). We thank Érudit for their support and access to data.
